comments:
This paper presents a practical system for generating Chinese classical quatrains using RNN Encoder-Decoder architectures with bidirectional gated units and attention. The authors decompose the task into three blocks (word-to-line, line-to-line, context-to-line), train on a large corpus, and introduce two notable techniques: inverting target lines during training to improve attention (motivated by particular syntactic/attributive properties of Classical Chinese), and combining a neural language model to increase idiomaticity. Evaluation includes automatic BLEU-2 scoring against automatically extracted references and a fairly extensive human evaluation by 16 expert raters; reported results indicate clear improvements over an SMT baseline and competitive performance close to human (poet) lines in some criteria.

Strengths:
- A well-motivated application of sequence-to-sequence models to a culturally and linguistically challenging creative task.
- Large training corpus and multiple model variants (SPB, CPB, WPB), with qualitative analyses (line representations, gated-unit behavior, attention visualizations) that help interpret what the model learns.
- Human evaluation by domain experts using multiple criteria, not just automatic metrics.
- Interesting empirical finding that inverting target lines improves attention and generation quality in this setting.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Important experimental and modeling details are missing or under-specified: hyperparameters (embedding and hidden sizes), optimization details, training epochs, regularization, vocabulary handling, and exact mechanism for enforcing/assessing tonal and rhyming constraints. The claim that 95% of outputs obey tonal constraints deserves an explicit description of how constraints are represented and enforced (or how conformity is measured).
- Baselines and comparisons could be stronger and better controlled. The SMT baseline adaptation and training data limitations are acknowledged by the authors; comparisons with other recent neural methods (e.g., Zhang & Lapata 2014) are discussed but not always included in quantitative comparison. Statistical significance of reported improvements is not provided.
- The BLEU reference construction (automatically extracting 20 references per line) is practical but may bias evaluation; more discussion of this method’s validity and limitations would be helpful.
- Reproducibility: the paper does not state data/code release. Given the custom corpus and cultural specifics, providing datasets, code, or at least full training/configuration details would be important.
- Some evaluation design details are unclear: how the human evaluators were assigned, inter-annotator agreement, and whether scores were averaged across raters with variance reported.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add full model/hyperparameter and training details, as well as the precise procedure used to check tonal/rhyme constraints.
- Include ablation experiments isolating the contributions of attention, target inversion, neural LM fusion weight, and the three-block architecture with statistical tests.
- Strengthen baselines (retrain Zhang & Lapata style models and other recent approaches) and report significance tests.
- Release code and (where possible) processed datasets or clear instructions for reconstruction to aid reproducibility.
- Report inter-annotator agreement on human evaluation and include example outputs in the appendix for qualitative inspection.

Overall, the paper makes a solid contribution to neural poetry generation and sequence-to-sequence modeling for semantically relevant sequences. With more complete methodological details and somewhat stronger comparative evaluation, it would be a clear acceptable contribution to the venue.
